text:
Olympics



LOUISVILLE, Ky. — She grabbed her pink water bottle, gave the uneven bars a quick spritz, climbed up and began her routine.Sunisa Lee, thedefending Olympic all-around gold medalistin women’s gymnastics, made it all look effortless and seamless, including twice executing the full-twisting layout Jaeger catch-and-release move that she hopes eventually will be named after her.





A post shared by Sunisa Lee (@sunisalee)



In truth, it was anything but effortless and seamless. The morning podium training workout here prior to Saturday’s Winter Cup was Lee’s first public workout since last year’s national championship in August, which came five months after she was diagnosed with an incurable kidney disease. The disease, which she has not identified by name, came on suddenly, swelling her joints and causing rapid weight gain that made it impossible to compete, shelved her elite competition and ended her plans to continue herNCAAcareer at Auburn.

Advertisement

Lee went from dreaming of chasing another all-around medal to simply hoping she could compete again as doctors first worked to accurately diagnose her condition and then find the proper medication cocktail to keep it at bay. The toll on her body was enormous, but her psyche took an even bigger hit.

“I think that took a little setback when I got sick,” Lee said Friday. “I got nervous. I wasn’t sure I was good enough to make theOlympics.”

She said she wasn’t in the “greatest mindset.” Her coach, Jess Graba, is much more direct.

“She’s been very depressed all year,” he said, using the word three times to describe the athlete he’s coached since she was 6.

The two have weathered plenty of storms before, Lee’s career beset by injuries and her Olympic journey delayed by the global pandemic, but nothing compares to this. Graba termed the year “terrible,” and admits he at times felt helpless trying to figure out how to help Lee navigate her illness.

“Getting her in the gym, that was the hardest part,” he said. “Get her out of depression and into the gym. At first I didn’t even want her to do gymnastics. I just wanted her to come and hang out, do stuff.”

Progress, once measured in literal leaps and bounds, instead came in baby steps. As Lee felt stronger, she frequented the gym more, using her body to gauge how hard she could work. “Fumbling around a little bit in the dark,” is how Graba describes the early days of navigating Lee’s health challenges with her gymnastics training.

In January, doctors gave her an all-clear — Lee is in remission now, she says — and provided Lee with a daily medication regimen that should manage the disease. Graba opted to divide and conquer the goals, giving Lee an immediate to-do list while he plotted a direction to a more long-term plan.

She will compete only on beam and bars here, the first because she has a comfort with it, the second because she wants to pursue a chance to have the skill named after her. Aiming for that became the temporary placeholder as Lee approached her more methodical approach to gymnastics once she got healthy. For it to happen, Lee will need to make the team at the Baku World Cup in March — only once successfully performed in an international event can a skill be named for a particular gymnast.

Advertisement

Her odds seem good, considering how she looked Friday and reading the tea leaves from Chellsie Memmel, the national team coach.

“I love seeing gymnastics continue to push these boundaries,” said Memmel, who herself has a move named after her. “If you can be the first one to perform and do it successfully, it’s just a cool feeling.”

That Lee can even pursue such goals a mere six months after returning to full-time training speaks to Lee’s competitive spirit and mental toughness. It is not easy. She still gets tired. She’s sore. And yet she’s here.

The big question, of course, is if that same mental attack will pay off with a spot on the Paris Olympics team.

“When she makes her mind up to do something, that’s usually what it is,” Graba says. “It doesn’t have a lot to do with us or anyone else. When Suni wants something, when Suni sets her mind to it, that’s usually what happens.”

Jordan Chiles stepped up at the Tokyo Olympics — now it's time for Paris

Dana O’Neil, a contributor to The Athletic, has worked for more than 25 years as a sports writer, covering the Final Four, the Super Bowl, World Series, NBA Finals and NHL playoffs. She has worked previously at ESPN and the Philadelphia Daily News. She is the author of three books, including "The Big East: Inside the Most Entertaining and Influential Conference in College Basketball History."Follow Dana on Twitter@DanaONeilWriter
title: Sunisa Lee, after ‘terrible’ 2023, takes her next big step toward another Olympics - The Athletic
author: Dana O'Neil
description: Since winning gold in Tokyo in 2021, the road has been anything but easy for Suni Lee. But now she's back with a new move and eyes on Paris.
keywords: College Sports, Olympics, Top Sports News
